ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general rice noodle dish class; no specific Ipoh Hor Fun entry exists. Closest defensible parent class used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked rice noodles as a category; no Ipoh-specific entry exists. Closest defensible parent class used at the rice noodle dish level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Ipoh Hor Fun or rice noodle soup dishes at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Ipoh Hor Fun is approximately 400 g, comprising flat rice noodles, broth, bean sprouts, poached chicken or prawns, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Ipoh Hor Fun — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010184 is sourced directly from HPB Foodo with provenance marked canonical and confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Ipoh Hor Fun exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 61 is estimated from the closest analogous food: rice noodles (fresh, cooked), GI approximately 61 (medium), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × (25.19 × 4)) / 100 = 61.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; a 400 g bowl delivers approximately 924 mg sodium, representing 40–46% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg, partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g (1308 mg per bowl).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request low-sodium broth, avoid adding soy sauce or dark soy condiments, and limit portion to one bowl per meal to keep total sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Flat rice noodles carry a medium-to-high GI (~61); a 400 g bowl provides approximately 100 g carbohydrate, which can produce a significant postprandial glucose excursion without adequate protein and fibre co-ingestion.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to 150–200 g of noodles, pair with protein toppings (poached chicken or prawns) and bean sprouts to slow glucose absorption, and monitor postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load from broth and condiments is the primary cardiovascular concern; cholesterol is reported as 0 mg/100 g and total fat is moderate at 4.21 g/100 g, but saturated fat breakdown is not available from the HPB source.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request minimal broth, avoid fried toppings or lard-based garnishes, and choose lean protein toppings (steamed chicken, prawns) to keep saturated fat and sodium within cardioprotective limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100 g supports gut microbiome immune modulation; low saturated fat and zero reported trans fat present minimal pro-inflammatory risk.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request anti-inflammatory toppings such as ginger broth, leafy greens, or bean sprouts, and avoid large portions that may drive low-grade inflammation via high glycaemic load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ipoh Hor Fun is a celebrated Singaporean and Malaysian Chinese rice noodle dish originating from Ipoh, Perak, Malaysia, and widely enjoyed across Singapore. It features silky, smooth flat rice noodles (hor fun / 河粉) served in a clear, aromatic chicken or prawn broth that is characteristically light yet deeply flavourful, often enriched with sesame oil and soy sauce. The dish is typically garnished with poached chicken slices, fresh prawns, bean sprouts, and spring onions. The noodles are prized for their delicate, slippery texture and mild flavour that absorbs the broth well. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with a notable dietary fibre content of 3.7 g and moderate sodium of 231 mg. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 61 (medium), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is suitable for most dietary patterns when consumed in moderate portions, though the broth significantly increases total sodium intake. Clinically, patients with hypertension should request low-sodium broth and avoid condiments; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should pair noodles with protein and vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose response; and patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid lard-based garnishes and excess broth. Ipoh Hor Fun is a popular hawker and restaurant dish consumed at lunch and dinner across Singapore and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: All core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The input record is marked canonical with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (rice noodles, cooked) in the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Ipoh Hor Fun preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (cr_id G00010184) as of April 2026. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB Foodo; this is a confirmed zero per the approved source for this dish preparation. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles (fresh, cooked) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Ipoh Hor Fun exists in any approved GI source. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = 61 × (25.19 × 4) / 100 = 61.5. The input alias 'Add Ipoh Hor Fun (Rice Noodle) 加沙河粉' reflects a menu-style prefix ('Add') from the HPB source record and is preserved in aliases for retrieval continuity. The canonical food_name has been normalised to 'Ipoh Hor Fun (Rice Noodle Soup)'. Cultural classification updated from 'Malay' to 'Singaporean Chinese / Malaysian Chinese' to accurately reflect the dish's Cantonese-Chinese origin from Ipoh, Perak.
